source.title: Long-term stabilization of place cell remapping produced by a fearful experience

confidence_rationale: Confirms and extends Moita 2004: fear-induced remapping persists for days, and the new fear-context representation stabilizes long-term. Adds the persistence dimension that map-tagging is consolidated, not transient.
failure_signatures: Fear-induced remapping is not a transient state-modulation; the new representation stabilizes long-term.
If REE assumed affect-driven remapping was a within-episode phenomenon only, this falsifies that assumption: the map carries the affective tag forward.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Recording from the same CA1 place cells across days of predator-odor contextual fear conditioning, a subset of cells changed their preferred firing locations during the fearful experience, and the new firing patterns stabilized in the long term.
mapping.ree_translation: The hippocampal map's affect-channel tagging is not a transient state phenomenon. Once a context acquires fear/threat-stream loading, the remapped representation persists, which is the architectural feature you'd expect of a stream that carries forward into future planning, simulation, and replay.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Predator-odor conditioning recruits innate-threat pathways (PAG, central amygdala) that may not be the same as shock-based fear used by Moita 2004 — the convergence of results across paradigms strengthens rather than weakens the cross-paradigm claim, but the inferred neuromodulatory pathway might differ.
mapping.source_context: Mice, dorsal CA1 single-unit recording across multiple days, predator-odor (TMT or fox urine analog) contextual fear conditioning.

confidence_components.notes: Cross-day recording is methodologically demanding and adds the persistence claim that Moita 2004 could not address. Confidence is bounded by sample size and the predator-odor paradigm specifics, but the result converges with Moita and is internally consistent.
